presently today that won't be captured in this.
But a redress package, um, for them may include
a, the, the right rehabilitative or, um, health
or social services, um, support to,
to address their criminogenic or health
or mental health reasons causing them to offend.
So are you saying or, or, or, and, and
I'm saying we expand those that get sent to a,
to greater scrutiny for redress.
So if, if, if we were to,
if the bill goes forward, then all persons
who have a criminal history should go to further, um,
for further scrutiny to ensure that the, the best package
of redress to support them
and to a bright future is able to be offered.
Excellent. Thank you. Uh, thank you.
I, um, I know you mentioned in your, in your submission,
but do you believe that excluding survivors of abuse
and care from accessing redress
because they've been sentenced
for a crime is double jeopardy?
I do. Which is why I'm not saying to exclude them.
I'm saying that there needs to be a mechanism to ensure
that, um, the package is able
to address their nuanced needs.
Right. Thank. Thanks, Aaron. Yeah. Oh yeah.
No, I think, um, thank you
for making a submission this afternoon,
um, or this evening rather.
And, uh, look, uh, thank you for coming in on,
on your holiday and wish you all the best with the,
uh, remainder of your holiday.
Sure. Done You right.
Now, um, last
but not least minute, uh, also
by Zoom is Andrew Godard this evening.
Uh, Andrew, welcome. Uh, thank you for, uh, staying online.
We, we have been running a little bit over.
Uh, we're trying to make sure everyone gets
a chance to, uh, be heard.
So look, thank you for preparing for us this afternoon.
The time is yours and when you are ready.
Um, thank you for the opportunity to speak
to my submission, which I'm making in a personal capacity.
I'm conscious, I'm, I'm last, so I'll try to be brief.
I just wanna admit here quickly to those who have spoken
before me, uh,
and in particular the survivors who have presented today.
I do not support the bill.
It is inconsistent with the Royal Commission's considered
recommendations relating to redress
and in particular that the entire redress scheme should be
independent and open to all survivors,
including those with the criminal record.
And I also want to stress
that the bill will not just affect the estimated 5%
of survivors who have committed serious
offenses as defined in the bill.
It will and it will disproportionately so,
but not exclusively.
It will affect every survivor who applies
because every survivor must consent
to a criminal record check
and make a declaration as to their criminal history.
Uh, which seems to me to be completely unnecessary
and not to mention completely unfair.
Uh, however, on the assumption that the bill is likely
to proceed, I'll focus my time on two,
maybe three issues I consider need urgent attention.
Uh, first pause.
19 of the bill provides
that the redress officer may only determine
that an affected survivor is eligible
for financial redress if satisfied
that it would not bring the redress scheme into disrepute.
The policy rationale, as I understand it, is
that there needs to be independent consideration in these
cases to avoid, to bring, bring the scheme into disrepute.
Uh, however it doesn't follow
that the test needs to use that language.
Exactly. Uh, disrepute test
is more commonly used in regulating professional conduct,
and I submit that it's ill suited as a test
to whether a remedy should be made available.
Uh, my submission is that if the bill is to proceed,
that the presumption should be reversed
and that the test should be whether withholding the payment
is in the interests of justice.
After all, this is fundamentally a question of justice.
Uh, I would also like to see the matters.
Uh, the officer must consider, um,
include information relating
to the abuse the survivor suffered, uh,
and including the trauma they have had to live with since.
And at the moment, it does not, uh, that
or those changes would retain the independent consideration,
I think the bill is trying to achieve, uh,
while offering a test that is less likely
to unjustly restrict access to financial redress
for those affected survivors.
Um, the second point I want to speak to is that clause eight
of the bill applies most
of the bills operative provisions retrospectively to nine,
Retrospective application of the law requires good reasons.
And the only rationale
provided in this case that I'm aware of is
that the government announced the core policy
of the bill on that date.
And frankly, that is completely inadequate
as a justification for retrospective retrospective law.
It also, uh, as the Law Society has pointed out,
gives improper weight to a public statement
made by the executive.
So my recommendation would be
to remove clause eight entirely.
Uh, however, I am,
I'm most concerned about the retrospective application
of the offense provisions and clauses 23
and 24 retrospective offense provisions are about
as serious a breach of fundamental constitutional principles
and of the Bill of Rights Act, as you could imagine.
Uh, so,
sorry, I seem to have frozen. I'm not sure.
Yeah, we got you back now.
Oh, pretty good.
Not sure it's entirely working with working for me,
but I think that the point I'm really trying to make is
that, yeah, that those respective, um, uh,
offense provisions really need to be removed from the bill.
I know the law society has, I think suggested, uh,
uh, rather than, than
that removing the offense provisions in their entirety, uh,
I would also support that as a means
for addressing my concerns.
Uh, and I think I'll, I think I'll leave it there, uh,
if indeed I'm still with you.
Yep. No, no, we, uh, we still do have you.
So I think, I think we've got,
I think we've got all the points you've made and,
and we do have your comprehensive written submission
as well, uh, which has been helpful.
Um, so look, I thank you.
We do one quick question, if anyone has one.
Um, otherwise we think we will wrap it up for the evening.
Alright. We don't, um, look, I think, I think you have, um,
uh, yeah, made those points very clearly
and we do have written submission.
Um, and thank you for yeah, very clear,
um, oral submission as well.
Just major points very clearly. So appreciate it.
No, you're welcome. Thank you. And good luck. Thank you.
Okay, good evening. And it's frozen again.
Alright, well that concludes our, um, hearing
for this evening.
